drivers: the prior forward path failed at target point 21 through corrector oscillation
the prior reverse path ended at its 30-point ceiling rather than a reviewed endpoint minimum
both directions must remain comparable and independently reviewed

common_constraints.force_constants: RCFC from the previously audited accepted TS Hessian
common_constraints.geometry_and_guess: Geom=AllCheck and Guess=Read from the audited accepted TS state
common_constraints.scf: Tight convergence with XQC fallback and 128 cycles
common_constraints.thermochemistry: not applicable to the IRC stage
common_constraints.low_frequency_policy: not applicable to the IRC stage
common_constraints.endpoint_policy: A maximum-point structure is not a minimum; chemical-side assignment and endpoint validation remain separate gates.

options.purpose: Lower-cost diagnostic rerun that reduces the path step modestly while retaining the prior corrector-cycle ceiling.

applicability.reason: Tests whether a modest step reduction removes the point-21 oscillation at limited extra cost.

method_profiles.relativistic_treatment: not required for B, C, F, H, N, and O
method_profiles.software_compatibility: reviewed for Gaussian 16 A.03 main-group closed-shell execution

acceptance_checks: corrector convergence at every completed point
manual endpoint structure review before any chemical-side assignment

validation_plan.claim_limit: Diagnostic only; the shorter and coarser path is not the primary endpoint-coverage protocol.

assumptions: Wall time remains dominated by path behavior rather than resource size.

limitations: The modest step reduction may not eliminate the prior forward oscillation.
The total path coverage may be shorter than needed for endpoint interpretation.
provenance: Complete forward and reverse terminal logs from the first bidirectional attempt.
User-approved omega-B97XD/6-31G(d), SMD toluene model for this benchmark.

options.purpose: Primary matched bidirectional rerun that reduces the step size enough to address corrector oscillation and extends both paths beyond the previous 30-point ceiling without merely increasing corrector cycles.

applicability.reason: Directly targets the observed numerical instability while keeping the original integrator, force constants, electronic structure, and corrector-cycle ceiling fixed.

method_profiles.relativistic_treatment: not required for B, C, F, H, N, and O
method_profiles.software_compatibility: reviewed for Gaussian 16 A.03 main-group closed-shell execution

acceptance_checks: corrector convergence at every completed point
matched numerical settings in the opposite direction
manual endpoint structure review before any chemical-side assignment

minimum_acceptance: numerically complete matched forward and reverse paths
validation_plan.claim_limit: Primary numerical path evidence only; endpoint minima and reaction-path validation remain separately gated.

coverage_plan.intended_use: matched bidirectional recalculation with extended path coverage

assumptions: The two full-node jobs may queue or run sequentially depending on capacity.

drivers: smaller step size than the first attempt

limitations: A smaller HPC step does not guarantee convergence through a genuinely bifurcating or very flat region.
Reaching 60 points still does not prove that the final geometry is a minimum.
provenance: Complete first-attempt logs show point-21 forward corrector oscillation rather than SCF or resource failure.
The successful reverse run shows stable HPC behavior through 30 points at the same electronic-structure model.

options.purpose: Finer matched bidirectional HPC sampling with the same total nominal path reach as standard but 90 smaller steps, retaining the original corrector-cycle ceiling to avoid concealing divergence.

applicability.reason: Provides a finer discretization sensitivity test without changing to LQA, EulerPC, DVV, or another integrator family.

method_profiles.relativistic_treatment: not required for B, C, F, H, N, and O
method_profiles.software_compatibility: reviewed for Gaussian 16 A.03 main-group closed-shell execution

acceptance_checks: corrector convergence at every completed point
matched fine sampling in the opposite direction
manual endpoint structure review before any chemical-side assignment

minimum_acceptance: numerically complete finely sampled forward and reverse paths
validation_plan.claim_limit: Numerical step-size sensitivity only; strict is not a guarantee of the chemical model or endpoint identity.

assumptions: Finer sampling increases gradients and wall time even though the nominal path reach is similar to standard.

limitations: The substantially higher cost may not add useful chemistry if standard already yields smooth, reviewable endpoints.
The same HPC integrator can still fail in a bifurcating or pathological region.
provenance: Finer step-size sensitivity is proposed in response to the observed forward corrector oscillation.
No alternate IRC integrator is introduced without a separate scientific-method approval.

comparison_notes: All three candidates keep the electronic-structure model, solvent, grid, SCF treatment, RCFC source, HPC integrator, recorrection policy, and 40-cycle corrector ceiling fixed.
The primary differences are IRC step size and the number of points needed to preserve or extend nominal path coverage.
Forward and reverse require separate hash-bound selections and exact live approvals even when the same tier is chosen.
non_claims: Strict is not an accuracy guarantee and does not validate the chemical model.
No candidate assigns forward or reverse to reactant or product before endpoint structure review.
No candidate proves an endpoint minimum merely by reaching its maximum point count.
